sentences.text: Well, I went over to assistant commandant, [?
Kiever, ?] and I told him.
They should me take down to the last route to be killed.
So he asked me how long I'm working there.
I said, quite a few weeks.
So he told me, go over to the doctor, Dr.
Cherenzitzki, ?] and he assigned me to go to the kitchen and to have some hot tea.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
So how I got in touch with Dr.
Cherenzitzki, ?] I spoke to him, very, very fine, nice gentleman.
What did he do for you?
Huh?
The kidney infection, there was another story about it.
When I got back, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and [?
Kiever ?] told me to go in in the barrack, and to rest up, for the first time in the history of Treblinka.
You take somebody who works there, and tell him to go-- to lay down and rest up.
Nobody wanted to believe me.
I had to call him over there, working.
And I don't know how he managed at the daytime to go back to that barrack where I was.
And just to open up the door and mention my name, but he did.
And I told him I'm alive.
At night when the job finished outside and people came in, and everybody on top of me.
What, how?
They couldn't believe that I'm still alive.
Over there, there was a doctor, Dr.
Mizarek. ?] Who is that?
From-- from Czechoslovakia.
No.
He wasn't a doctor.
He was a worker just like me, like anybody.
He wasn't a doctor.
The doctor there was only doctor [?
Cherenzitzki. ?] Dr.
Mizarek ?] came over.
He said to me, what happened?
And I told him.
He took out a tablet.
He said, here, take the tablet.
If wouldn't help you, tell me.
I came over.
I got that tablet, swallow it.
And what can I tell you?
Thank god.
Up till now, I never had another attack, and I never felt anything.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's unbelievable.
But that's the truth, what had happened.
You mentioned you were also assigned to cutting the women's hair.
Yes.
Yes.
That worked like that.
When the transport come, and at one time, they called up they needed barbers.
I was a barber, not a bad one.
Women's hair, men's hair, and we got some more barbers.
Even a couple of people there were not barbers at all.
And they assigned us to cut off the hair of the women before they were gassed.
They had not place yet.
So that was the first time they took in people from camp number I to camp number II; to the extermination camp, which the camp number II was the extermination camp.
Yes.
And we went in, and they told us what to do.
We have benches.
The women were all naked.
They were sitting on the benches.
And we had to do our job.
They shouldn't be suspicious that they're going to be killed.
So how to cut off their hair, to make believe they're going to take a shower, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] which we did.
Yes.
Scissors, common scissors, no clippers, just a common scissor.
We took a bunch of hair.
We cut with these scissors.
And we let it on the floor.
After the transport was finished, we went out.
First, it was when we were finished with the job, cutting the woman's hair, they told us to step out from the gas chamber, and they put in gas.
They gassed them.
Inside the gas chamber, yes.
That's what we had to pass.
Have to go there, we had to pass that, that row.
Some.
I tell you.
I tell you, first I want to say that after we were finished with one transport, we came out from the gas chamber.
Another transport went in.
And we went in again, we did the job.
If you're asking a question about we had a conversation, yes.
I had a conversation with a girl I will never forget her name.
She was from a little town by the name of [INAUDIBLE].
Her name was Chana Levinson.
I never know the girl, the most gorgeous girl I knew.
And so she said, listen.
You are a young man.
Try escape from here, and let the world know what's going on here.
That's exactly her words.
And that's how long it took cutting her hair.
Because we couldn't stay for 10-15 minutes with one girl or with one woman.
Which that was my conversation with it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Otherwise, we were not allowed.
They were not allowed to talk to us.
We were not allowed to talk to them.
We were about 16 barbers.
Whenever-- whenever the transport came, we were assigned to that job.
When this transport was finished, we were sent back to camp number II, where we did our usually working day by selecting clothes.
No.
No.
We were outside.
We didn't see that.
Why?
I tell you why.
The entrance see, was from this side.
When they took out the bodies, was from the other side of the gas chamber.
Yes.
They took those bodies to a huge pit, which a machine was working, taking out the ground, making pits.
And they were taken over to those places, and they were buried.
That was at one time.
There was another time when they burned dead bodies.
One top of the-- no.
At that time, I wasn't there.
Yes.
Well, I experienced a fact when a transport came from a little town by the name of Parczew.
A woman came out from the train.
And she went over to a man.
He looked like a German.
He act like a German.
She had with her a razor, and cut off his throat.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yes.
I don't know.
And that was two of them she did.
One of them was-- I know him.
Because he was a barber from Warsaw.
Salek was his name, cut the other throats too.
But the first one was serious.
The Germans, they took him to a hospital, a special hospital for special Germans.
They tried to heal him, to bring him back to life.
Boy, he passed away at the hospital.
They brought back the body to Treblinka.
And that was the only funeral which I myself, and more people I talk to them, have never seen a Jewish funeral organized by the German with all the honors.
He was Jewish, yes.
Because, to, to their definition he was a hero, working for the-- for the German.
And he was some kind-- he was the head man from the gas chambers.
He was a Jew.
Yes.
In Treblinka.
The Germans.
They order to--
No.
The Germans ordered him to bury.
There was another, in fact, also there.
While we were working, they got two people that tried to escape Treblinka.
One of them was a kid from my hometown, a kid.
At that time, he was about 20, 22 years old.
They brought him back to Treblinka.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They erected a gallow.
And they hanged him by the legs.
They were hanging by their legs from about 9:00, 9:30 in the morning, until about 5:00, 6 o'clock at night.
But this young kid by the name of Langener, he was so brave.
He didn't cry. that we should rescue him.
His only wish was run away from here, and tell the world what's going on here, what had happened to our Jewish people.
They-- at night, the head-- everything from the body went into the head.
The heads were like they're swollen.
Because the inside went to the head.
At night, they, they killed him.
They shot him.
Number I.
This was in camp number I.
Yes.
Yes, I was beaten at one day, I was beaten three times.
Why, because from the food, from other thing I got a skin sickness, which was layered out, pieces by pieces, and full of pus.
Which nobody could stay near me, even where we stayed, go in for coffee, or to be in row one of the-- it was hurting that much.
So I just leaned a little bit on the outside of the row.
And I got beaten.
First time, he beat me, a Ukrainian.
Now the second time I got hit by [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [?
Rakoski. ?]
The camp kapo, yes.
And the third time I was-- I got beaten by another kapo.
That was one time, one day, three times a day, in all mine concentration history that I got beaten.
Over the head.
That's what they hit, over the head.
What happened to me after that, we had an illegal organization between our own people.
There was involved, people I know from my hometown.
There were involved also people from Warsaw, which I know the names from my hometown.
But I don't know the names from other.
Because we didn't give names like, we know by the first name.
[INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], Lutig, something like that.
From my hometown, there were quite a few people, which was including myself, was a cousin of mine, Goldstein, a man by the name of Zaidman, a man by the name of Kaufman.
And our aim was to run away.
No.
We couldn't have a meeting.
We do at night, during the night, we sleeped close to each other.
So we talked to each other.
Let's do.
But to do this, we had to organize to have money, a lot of money.
Outside everything what you wanted to do, or you're supposed to do, was involved with money.
For money we could even buy some of the Gestapo.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So it happened one night we went from the barrack out on our bellies [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] near the-- the gate.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] All of a sudden, Ukrainian jumped on us.
Some of us-- we were about seven or eight of us-- went back to the barrack.
What had happened?
Then we find out.
They find the first two guys with a lot of money.
They took the money away, and they let the guys off.
They took away all the money.
The following day, the Ukrainian disappeared with the money.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But it came over there, there was everything, even with Jewish people, were such kind that the German knew everything what's going on.
We have between our Jewish people also, betrayers.
Informers, right.
No, we didn't know it.
But they were.
After that, we, we know it is.
And when the Germans find out there was supposed to be an escape from some people, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they tried to get some people off it.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And when another escape, from three people, also one of them from our group escaped with them-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and what had happened after 10, 12 days, he came back to the camp.
For what I don't know.
When I spoke to him, he said, he came back to show the road how to escape from Treblinka, and to get in-- into Warsaw and other places.
And came back, yes.
Through the gate.
They didn't had an accurate list of people.
Because, let's say, from the barrack when they went to work, 400 people.
At least 25, 30 got shot.
So the exact list, the exact name they didn't had.
Yes.
His name was Rosenthal.
His first name was Lebo.
Yeah.
That's what he said.
I wouldn't say.
I don't know.
Maybe came back to, to take money.
I don't know.
But he was in touch with the-- with the commandant.
And he was having a meeting with the commandant all night in the barrack.
And he told the commandant how to escape through barbed wires.
No.
The Jewish, Galewski.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A day after he got from Galewski, and he got from kapo [?
Rakoski ?] a lot on money.
And he escaped again.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Well, it happened to him when he was in the woods around Warsaw, they caught, and they brought him back to Treblinka.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He went in right to the gas chamber.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Because the story I know exactly.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That what had happened, it-- that I was involved.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was sleeping near him, and a few more, two cousins of mine.
A day after he escaped, they came over to [?
Rakoski, ?] [INAUDIBLE].
He said, we know there was-- there was a man escaped.
He came back.
He was with you all night.
Rakoski ?] said, no.
I don't know anything about it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He started calling out names.
He called up five names.
He called my name was also to come over.
I came over.
And this Galewski, [INAUDIBLE] Galewski--
Camp elder, camp commandant, we called him.
The Jewish, yes.
He said to [?
Kiever ?] that this is the barber, not him.
He let me go.
They took in the six men to the lazaret.
Lazaret is another place, a hospital.
The lazaret was a place planted with trees around.
It couldn't be visible.
And inside, like a door, they went in.
And it was a very a huge pit where they killed people.
Let's say, like, we'd come from a transport, crippled, children or small children, they got lost in the [INAUDIBLE].
We, the people who worked there, we had to carry those children or those crippled people into that place, which is called lazaret.
And a man in a white, short or long one, with a red cross, his name was [?
Kurland. ?] He was the head from over there.
And they killed those kids or those crippled people.
That in the camp number I, outside the camp number I-- not outside, actually it was inside, but away from the place where we had the barracks.
So at that time, me and one cousin of mine, a friend, we decided to divide in groups by 12 people, three of them each time to escape.
Two groups escaped before us.
The first group came in, went through, and went into Czestochowa.
They were two brothers.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And there were informed by some Jewish people.
They took them away, and they killed them.
The other group [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was alive.
They came also back.
One of them is still alive.
He lives in Israel.
Yeah.
Oh, no, no.
The only fact, that's the only one, the only one in the history of Treblinka that he come back.
He was the only one, yes.
And stayed out, some of them.
Like, I escaped with a cousin of mine.
And we got to Czestochowa.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And what had happened, we went into the ghetto.
Then we went into-- to the concentration camp.
And they took him out to Buchenwald.
And he got killed over there in Buchenwald.
Yes.
It was my time.
And--
We organized the place.
Now the place we organized was in the barrack, between the bundles of clothes.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now, what we did, we divided the clothes, like it was, let's say, two or three stories high.
We make like a hole, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we covered ourselves with bundles from the top.
When the German went in, with their ammunition, and they tried, you know, to stick those picks-- that we-- he couldn't get us.
So we had to be very deep in that hole.
It was a time, it was very hard.
But the main thing was, and it happened which we find many people that couldn't breathe, or the bundle just gots from one place to the other place.
And to go out, was impossible.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
You understand what I said?
It was very horrible.
In that same barrack, in the time I worked, we took out over 30 people killed.
They died in the bundles?
They died.
From they had no air to breathe, because the bundles [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] moved from place-- while they went down, they moved from one place to the other, and they covered them.
Yes.
No, no, no.
Nobody could do for us.
Once you went out in hiding, you couldn't go back to the barrack.
They was all, you dead or you disappear.
Go back to the barrack?
That was no way to do it.
And nobody did it.
We decided after they killed those five people, and they let me off.
And I know they will-- eventually, they will take me too, and those two others.
Because we were in the group.
And we were sleeping together.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] At that time, I decided that I have to do something, something to do was to escape.
